body:
'empathy'. 

When I first started out as a designer, I read somewhere that a good designer needs to have empathy. At that point in my life, I had just realized that my true passion and strength lay in the creative world, only after burning two years of my life and tens of thousands of dollars of my parents' savings, so I didn't have time to reflect on that notion. 

And my work reflected that of myself. The designs from my career early on were all about visual spectacles. I didn't know any other way to increase the quality of a design. While many of these works were visually pleasing, they lacked rationale–and without rationale, any success was nearly impossible to replicate. Naturally, my career began to stagnate, with projects that were largely performative and carried little commercial value. 

Then I discovered a new realm of design-UI/UX: the art of designing creative solutions to real-world problems. Design with purpose and feasibility. Design with an answer.
